questionText: Explain why the equation $\sec x = \tfrac{1}{2}$ has no solutions for $0^\circ \le x < 360^\circ$.

steps.explanation: As usual we express secant as the reciprocal of cosine, so the equation can be tested against what cosine is able to do.
Taking reciprocals of both sides gives $\cos x = 2$; this is the value cosine would have to reach.
Cosine is always between $-1$ and $1$ for every real angle $x$; this bound is the heart of the argument.

steps.description: Compare the required value with the range
steps.explanation: The value $2$ lies outside the interval $[-1,1]$, so no angle can have a cosine of $2$.
Since cosine can never exceed $1$, the equation $\cos x = 2$ has no solutions at all, in any interval.

steps.description: Look at it through the range of secant
steps.explanation: Because cosine has magnitude at most $1$, its reciprocal has magnitude at least $1$. This gives the useful fact that $|\sec x|$ is always $\ge 1$.

steps.description: State the range restriction on secant
steps.explanation: Secant never takes any value strictly between $-1$ and $1$; its outputs sit outside that gap.

steps.description: Compare the target with that restriction
steps.explanation: The target value $\tfrac12$ has magnitude less than $1$, so it falls inside the forbidden gap and cannot be a secant value.

steps.description: Note the interval does not matter
steps.workingLatex: \text{no } x \text{ works, so none in } 0^\circ \le x < 360^\circ
steps.explanation: Since there is no solution for any $x$ at all, there is certainly none in the stated interval; restricting the interval cannot create a solution.
Both viewpoints agree: because $|\sec x| \ge 1$ always, the equation $\sec x = \tfrac12$ has no solutions in the given interval.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: There are no solutions. Rearranging gives $\cos x = 2$, which is impossible since $-1 \le \cos x \le 1$. Equivalently $|\sec x| \ge 1$ for all $x$, so $\sec x$ can never equal $\tfrac12$.
